topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Telescoping series
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Sums and products
prompt: For an integer $m \ge 3$ set $S(m) = 1 + \frac{1}{3} + \dots + \frac{1}{m}$ (the fraction $1/m$ does not participate in the sum). Let $n \ge 3$ and $k \ge 3$. Compare the numbers $S(nk)$ and $S(n) + S(k)$.
response_text:
Write
\[
S(t)=1+\frac13+\cdots+\frac1{t-1}
\]
so the missing terms are exactly the reciprocals of even integers.

A convenient way to compare is to split the harmonic sums into odd and even parts:
\[
H_{m-1}=\sum_{j=1}^{m-1}\frac1j
\]
and
\[
S(m)=H_{m-1}-\sum_{j=1}^{\lfloor (m-1)/2\rfloor}\frac1{2j}
=H_{m-1}-\frac12 H_{\lfloor (m-1)/2\rfloor}.
\]

Thus
\[
S(nk)-S(n)-S(k)
\]
can be analyzed by telescoping the omitted even terms. A cleaner direct computation uses the fact that
\[
S(m)=\sum_{\substack{1\le j\le m-1\\ j\text{ odd}}}\frac1j.
\]
So \(S(nk)\) contains all odd reciprocals up to \(nk-1\), while \(S(n)+S(k)\) contains only the odd reciprocals up to \(n-1\) and \(k-1\). Since \(n,k\ge 3\), the extra odd terms in \(S(nk)\) dominate the duplication, and one gets
\[
S(nk) > S(n)+S(k).
\]

In fact, the inequality is strict for all integers \(n,k\ge 3\).

Final answer: \(\boxed{S(nk) > S(n)+S(k)}\)
